DRIPS: Dynamic Rebalancing of Pipelined
Streaming Applications on CGRAs
Cheng Tan†, Nicolas Bohm Agostini†, Tong Geng†, Chenhao Xie†, Jiajia Li§, Ang Li†,
Kevin J. Barker†, Antonino Tumeo†
†Paciﬁc Northwest National Laboratory, §William & Mary
†{cheng.tan, nicolas.agostini, tong.geng, chenhao.xie, ang.li, kevin.barker, antonino.tumeo}@pnnl.gov,
§jli49@wm.edu
Abstract—Coarse-grained reconﬁgurable arrays (CGRAs) pro-
vide higher ﬂexibility than application-speciﬁc integrated circuits
(ASICs) and higher efﬁciency than ﬁne-grained reconﬁgurable
devices such as Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs).
However, CGRAs are generally designed to support ofﬂoading of
a single kernel. While the CGRA design, based on communicating
functional units, appears to naturally suit data streaming appli-
cations composed of multiple cooperating kernels, current ap-
proaches only statically partition the resources across application
kernels. However, emerging streaming applications at the edge
(scientiﬁc instruments, sensor networks, network processing)
perform much more than digital signal processing and often are
data and input dependent. This leads to extremely variable kernel
execution times, severely impacting the throughput of the entire
pipeline if resources are only statically allocated. Therefore, in
this paper, we propose DRIPS — a novel CGRA architecture
that can dynamically rebalance the pipeline of data-dependent
streaming applications. We present a uniﬁed compiler framework
to facilitate the mapping of a given streaming application onto
the DRIPS CGRA architecture. The experimental results show
that DRIPS achieves an average throughput improvement of
1.46× across a set of representative applications over a statically
partitioned solution. The additional area overhead to enable
dynamic rebalancing consumes 16.34% of the entire area for
a 5×5 CGRA prototype.
I. INTRODUCTION
Data stream processing applications, which apply complex
sequences of computational kernels to continuously generat-
ed/acquired data, are becoming ubiquitous. The Internet of
Things (IoT), data centers, and edge computing devices for
many different areas require very specialized accelerators to
deal with quickly changing streams of data, and typically
compute results with low latency under very speciﬁc power
constraints. For example, smart sensor networks (power grid,
urban pollution, sea monitoring) need very power-efﬁcient
ways to reduce data to transmit across relatively slow com-
munication links. Other systems, like large scientiﬁc instru-
ments (particle accelerators, electron microscopes) capture and
stream inordinate amounts of data that cannot be stored com-
pletely [4, 32] and thus require high throughput and extremely
low processing latency. One speciﬁc characteristic of these
data streams is that their characteristics are highly variable,
leading to highly different execution times for each of the
computational kernels of the processing pipeline.
This work was performed while Jiajia Li was afﬁliated with Paciﬁc
Northwest National Laboratory.
Accelerators based on reconﬁgurable devices have been
successfully employed to accelerate streaming applications,
speciﬁcally targeting image processing, convolutional neural
networks, and deep learning [3, 11, 27, 29, 34, 35, 45]. The
conventional approach focuses on maximizing the overall
throughput of the applications by accelerating all kernels si-
multaneously. Coarse-grained reconﬁgurable arrays (CGRAs),
loosely deﬁned as sets of functional units or interconnected
by a communication network on which a compiler can map
complex computational patterns deciding how the data ﬂows
through the system, appear a promising class of accelerators
for these speciﬁc application domains. Commonly used today
for digital signal processing (DSP) and, more in general,
multimedia applications [22, 40], they provide adaptability to
evolving algorithmic approaches thanks to their conﬁgurabil-
ity, but are more efﬁcient and faster to reconﬁgure than ﬁne-
grained conﬁgurable devices (such as ﬁeld-programmable gate
arrays). Their reﬁned communication networks enable efﬁcient
data communication from one processing element to the other,
allowing to deﬁne complex data processing pipelines with high
throughputs.
Although CGRAs seem appropriate for applications com-
posed of several communicating kernels, they are generally
programmed in two ways to support data streaming. The ﬁrst
approach uses a CGRA in its entirety for a single kernel
at a time and is reconﬁgured for each subsequent kernel.
In such a situation, if a kernel is small and does not have
enough parallelism, CGRA’s resources may remain unused.
In the second case, the CGRA is statically partitioned to
carefully assign hardware resources to all the kernels of the
application at the same time. Unfortunately, this works well
only if the performance (execution delay) of each kernel does
not change when the characteristics of the input data vary.
For example, edge devices in highly dynamic sensor networks
(monitoring stations for the power grid or pollution, or quickly
moving autonomous drones) may present sudden changes in
the sensed data when the environment changes that produce
a high variation of the execution times for the data ﬁltering
kernels, and consequently inﬂuence subsequent classiﬁcation
stages. Edge devices

the other. This leads to a highly variable degree of sparsity of
the data structures that then results in variable times for each
one of the kernels in the rest of the processing pipeline. Hence,
statically partitioning the CGRA may lead to different kernels
becoming the bottleneck in the processing pipeline, lowering
the overall throughput, and make the non-bottleneck kernels
underutilize the assigned resources.
To address these shortcomings, we propose DRIPS, a novel
CGRA design that enables Dynamic Rebalancing of pIPelined
Streaming applications. DRIPS implements partial dynamic
reconﬁguration based on the current pipeline execution status
to maximize the overall throughput. The speciﬁc contributions
of this paper are as follows:
• We propose DRIPS, a novel CGRA architecture (Sec-
tion III) that can dynamically rebalance the pipeline of
streaming applications based on the execution status to
achieve the maximized throughput;
• To facilitate the dynamic rebalancing, we develop the
ﬁrst uniﬁed framework (Section IV) to generate the
conﬁguration candidates of each kernel that will be used
to partially reconﬁgure the CGRA at runtime.
• We use a set of representative data-dependent streaming
applications to evaluate DRIPS (Section V). A 5×5
DRIPS prototype is implemented at both the cycle-level
and the register-transfer-level (RTL) to obtain perfor-
mance, power, area, and timing metrics.
II. BACKGROUND AND MOTIVATION
As brieﬂy introduced, CGRAs are either designed to support
the ofﬂoading of a single kernel in the application at a
time or statically partitioned among multiple communicating
kernels. The second approach seems more suitable for data
streaming applications to achieve the optimal throughput, but
requires kernels whose execution times do not change with
the characteristics of the data. The varying data characteristics
lead to sequences of kernels that can present signiﬁcantly
different execution times depending on the speciﬁc inputs.
Some examples are data analysis pipelines that perform graph
representation learning (i.e., graph neural networks, GNNs
[46]). GNNs organize their inputs in graph structures, which
may have signiﬁcantly varying features (not only the scale,
or size of the graphs in terms of the number of vertices,
but also their edge distribution, or sparsity), consequently
requiring very different processing times. Graph convolutional
networks (GCNs) are speciﬁc instances of GNNs where the
propagation module is a convolution operator that transforms
the graph into the spectral domain and aggregates information
from neighbors of vertices. In this section, we discuss the
acceleration on CGRA of a simple, but critical data-dependent
kernel of GCNs, sparse matrix-vector multiplication (SpMV)
in Section II-A and provide a full motivational example for
DRIPS with a GCN in Section II-B.
A. Kernel Acceleration on CGRAs

CGRAs of different sizes. For SpMV, the number of loop

map the SpMV onto a CGRA, the operations in the loop
body are ﬁrstly represented in a dataﬂow graph (DFG). This
could be easily generated from LLVM [16] IR. Subsequently,
each operation is assigned to a tile and scheduled to start at a
speciﬁc cycle [23]. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the optimal schedule
leads to the best initiation interval (II), which is 4 by using 2
CGRA tiles. This indicates that the schedule is repeated every
4 cycles, i.e, a new loop iteration can start every 4 cycles.
Thus, executing the entire SpMV requires 80 cycles. A 3-tile
CGRA can, instead, achieve a minimal II of 3, thus leading
to an execution time of 61 cycles for the entire loop.

B. Throughput-Oriented Acceleration for Streaming Applica-
tions
CGRAs, characterized by data ﬂowing from one processing
element to the other, have been demonstrated to be able
to reach high throughput for streaming applications. Xil-
inx Adaptable Intelligent (AI) Engines [10] in the Versal
device and the SambaNova Reconﬁgurable Dataﬂow Unit
(RDU) [1, 44] are two prominent examples of dataﬂow design
where a streaming application composed of multiple kernels is
represented in a single dataﬂow graph and remains ﬁxed during
its execution independently from variations of the input data.
Such a static hardware allocation approach does not allow the
accelerators to react to the variations of the inputs, leading
to an unbalanced pipeline and a reduction of the overall
throughput. In the following paragraphs, we use a GCN as
a motivating example to illustrate the effects of the variation
of the inputs and to explain how DRIPS improves the overall
throughput by dynamically rebalancing the pipeline of the
computational kernels.
Fig. 2(a) shows a 2-layer GCN used to classify a series
of enzymes (represented as graphs). The ﬁrst layer of the
network consists of an aggregate kernel (Aggregate1), a com-
bine kernel, and a ReLU kernel. The ReLU kernel is merged
with the combine kernel (CombineReLU) through loop fusion
because it is much smaller than the others (this is normally
done by machine learning compiler frameworks, such as
MLIR [15] or TVM [6]). The second layer is composed of an
aggregate kernel (Aggregate2), a combine kernel (Combine),
and a pooling kernel (Pooling).

The input graph of the GCN is usually represented as an
adjacency matrix that is stored in a compressed format (e.g.,
CSR, CSC, COO, etc) [18, 30] because of its inherent high
degree of sparsity (Compress). The aggregate kernels in GCN
collect multiple feature vectors from source neighbors into
one single feature vector and are executed through a sparse
matrix-matrix multiplication (SpMM). As shown in Fig. 1,
the execution time of the SpMV (i.e., the innermost loop of
the SpMM) varies with the number of nonzeros. On the other
hand, the combine kernels (i.e., Combine and CombineReLU)
perform dense general matrix-matrix multiplications (GEMM)
on the weight and feature matrix. Since the number of nodes
and degree of the edges in the input graphs representing the
enzymes might signiﬁcantly vary, the execution time of the
same kernel in the GCN may be different depending on the
actual input graph.
To enable high throughput on the CGRA for the GCN
inference, current approaches statically partition the CGRA
resources for each kernel (Fig. 2(c)) rather than allocating
all the resources to each kernel at one time (Fig. 2(b)).
The difference in execution cycles is illustrated in Fig. 3.
Unfortunately, the sparsity of the input matrices (caused by
different numbers of nodes and edges) for aggregate kernels
varies. This leads to an imperfect pipeline limited by different
kernels at different times as shown on the rightmost side of
Fig. 2(c). On the opposite, Fig. 2(d) demonstrates how DRIPS
improves the overall throughput by dynamically rebalancing
the pipeline. To be speciﬁc, the aggregate kernels exhibit
longer execution times on input matrices with high density
(i.e., larger graphs with more nodes and edges). When a
series of large input graphs arrives, Aggregate2 is limiting
the throughput while Compress is not, resources of Compress
could be migrated to Aggregate2 to balance the application
pipeline. That is, Aggregate2 is subject to an upgrade of
resources (Fig. 2(d)), and Compress is subject to a downgrade.
Resources assigned to Compress needs to be reduced by the
same amount of resources added to Aggregate2. In such a
way, the pipeline is rebalanced and the overall throughput
is improved (as shown in Fig. 3, the execution throughput
reduces from 47183 cycles/input to 30346 cycles/input).
III. DRIPS ARCHITECTURE
The overview of the DRIPS system architecture is illustrated
in Fig. 4. The DRIPS CGRA is loosely coupled with a
central processing unit (CPU) and can be invoked through the
accelerator command interface. The data and control signals
can be fed into the CGRA’s data scratchpad memory (SPM)
and control memory through a direct memory access (DMA)
unit. The current DRIPS prototype is composed of 25 (5×5)
tiles.
Novel Architectural Features – Differently from the con-
ventional mesh-based CGRA design, DRIPS tiles are inter-
connected in a king mesh (i.e., 8 directions) topology [20]. At
runtime, 4 directions out of the 8 are conﬁgured to reshape
the physical placement of a kernel into a logical placement
using the same number of tiles (detailed in Section III-C
and demonstrated in Fig. 5). This is powered by the two
additional crossbar switches and the Reconnector module (see
Fig. 6). Note that a logical placement means a mapping
that is statically generated for a kernel at compilation time,
while a physical placement indicates how the tiles form the
logical placement through dynamic rebalancing at runtime.
Moreover, DRIPS tiles integrate an additional network to
communicate control signals and execution status (presented in
Section III-B). As shown in Fig. 4(c), the components in gray
identify conventional components of a CGRA tile. The green
and blue elements, instead, highlight the new components
needed to implement a DRIPS tile. In addition, we propose the
DRIPS Controller to enable the dynamic rebalancing (detailed
in Section III-C).

A. DRIPS Tile
A DRIPS tile consists of a functional unit (FU), three sets of
registers, three crossbar switches, and a control memory. The
functional unit supports all the basic operations (e.g., add, mul,
shift, select, branch, load, store, etc). Tiles perform memory
operations (i.e., load/store) by directly accessing the data
memory (a global banked SPM) through the per-row shared
bus highlighted in yellow in Fig. 4(b). The data generated by a
functional unit are packed in a 34-bit word (32-bit for data, 1-
bit for predicate, and 1-bit for bypassing). Control dependency
is represented as data dependency and supported with partial
predication [12]. Each control signal is a word of 29 bits. A
14-bit ﬁeld is used to conﬁgure the functional unit at each
cycle, the other 15-bit ﬁeld contains routing information for
the 6×7 crossbar switch. The total size of the control memory
is 480 bytes, which is large enough for any kernel evaluated
in this work. At runtime, if a tile needs to be reallocated or its
interconnection modiﬁed to support a different mapping of a
kernel (reshaping of the logical placement), its control memory
is updated through the DRIPS Control Network (highlighted in
green in Fig. 4(c)). In addition, the Reconnector reconﬁgures
the 8×4 and 4×8 crossbar switches to effectively form the
new kernel logical placement as shown in Fig. 6.
B. DRIPS Control Network
The DRIPS Control Network transports the control signals
(29 bits), execution status (i.e., a 1-bit termination signal from
a tile and a 1-bit launch signal from the DRIPS Controller),
and the connection information to form a kernel logical place-
ment (i.e., 12 bits for each crossbar switch in a tile, for a total
of 24 bits). Therefore, the total size of a packet delivered by the
DRIPS Control Network is 55 bits. The network implements
an X-Y routing strategy to avoid deadlocks. Once a kernel
completes (i.e., a functional unit executes a branch that breaks
out the loop), the termination notiﬁcation is packaged up and
sent to the DRIPS Controller through the Control Network,
subsequently starting the execution of the dependent kernels.
C. DRIPS Controller
The DRIPS Controller keeps collecting the execution status
of each kernel in the exeTable by receiving the termination
signals from the DRIPS Control Network (Figure 4(d)). Mean-
while, the mapping between the kernels and the tiles is stored
and constantly updated in the mapTable. The depTable, resets
every time a new application starts, records the application’s
kernels dependencies, and issues launch requests to the DRIPS
Control Network. Once the pipeline is rebalanced (i.e., a
kernel is upgraded or downgraded), the DMA unit fetches the
corresponding control signals, assembles the control packets,
and issues them to the appropriate tiles via the DRIPS Control
Network.
The DRIPS Controller can trigger application pipeline re-
balancing after collecting the execution information during
a time window. A 10-round time window indicates that the
rebalancing is triggered once the last kernel completes its 10th
execution. We evaluate the effects of the length of the time
window in Section V-B (see Fig. 10).

Interaction with the Pipeline – The rebalancing mecha-
nism does not stop the pipeline execution as it actually works
on the mapTable, the exeTable, and the depTable to recognize
the appropriate conﬁgurations of the affected kernels. Those
tables indicate the current execution status of the pipeline.
However, loading the corresponding conﬁgurations (i.e., con-
trol signals) of the affected kernels delays the execution of the
dependent kernels. Fortunately, the overhead of transferring
the control signals is negligible compared to the entire pipeline
execution as detailed in Section V-B. In addition, loading
the conﬁguration does not pause the execution of the other
kernels that are running concurrently and independently from
the rebalanced ones. For example, loading the conﬁguration of
the second stage will delay the third stage but will not interfere
with the execution of the ﬁrst stage that will be processing the
next input instance. Loading the control signals for a speciﬁc
kernel can only happen after the current execution and before
its next launch. At that time, there are no valid values in the
allocated tiles or on the routed path. Instead, the SPM holds
the output of the current pipeline stage, ready to be processed
by the following stage. The exeTable records whether a kernel
is running or not.
D. DRIPS Scratchpad Memory
All the DRIPS tiles can access the global banked SPM
through the per-row shared bus. Bank conﬂicts are handled
by increasing execution delays of load/store operations. Since
the computational kernel is accelerated in a data-ﬂow fashion,
the longer latency of (pipelined) memory accesses does not
signiﬁcantly affect the overall speedup. Moreover, the DRIPS
Controller allows at most one memory access operation on
each row at each cycle.
The size of the SPM can signiﬁcantly affect the performance
of streaming applications, as discussed by many works [7, 19,
28, 33, 43]. The current DRIPS prototype contains a 4-bank
32KB data SPM, which is sufﬁcient for all the applications
evaluated in this work and allows supporting double-buffering.

grated framework is composed of four main parts: an MLIR
front-end, the Loop-Level Transformations, LLVM, and the
hardware backend.

B. Loop-Level Transformation
If there is an appropriate dialect to represent a streaming ap-
plication, loop-level transformation can directly be performed
in the MLIR framework. Otherwise, they are performed
through LLVM optimization passes. Transformations are as
follows:
Loop Flattening – if there are multiple nested loops, each
kernel in the rearranged pipeline is ﬂattened into a single-
nested loop [17]. This avoids multiple invocations of the
innermost loop and facilitates DFG Mapping.
Loop Unrolling – kernels with low instruction-level paral-
lelism cannot efﬁciently utilize the computing resources of
a large CGRA. Applying loop unrolling to extract higher
instruction-level parallelism and generate a larger DFG allows
reaching higher speedups if more hardware resources (i.e.
CGRA tiles) are provided [39]. However, this is not guaran-

DFG Generation – After applying the appropriate loop-
level transformations, the toolchain can generate the DFG of
each kernel. Each DFG node represents one basic operation
corresponding to

framework applies different unrolling factors for each kernel

C. LLVM Level
DFG Generation – After applying the appropriate loop-
level transformations, the toolchain can generate the DFG of
each kernel. Each DFG node represents one basic operation
corresponding to one instruction in LLVM intermediate repre-
sentation (IR). Control dependencies are supported by convert-
ing them to data dependencies using partial predication [12]
and providing hardware support for phi and branch operations.
DFG Mapping – DRIPS leverages a heuristic method [38]
to quickly map the control and dataﬂow graph (CDFG)
on all the partitions with different numbers of the CGRA
tiles. Speciﬁcally, on a 5×5 DRIPS CGRA, the compilation
framework could map the DFG of a kernel onto a 1-tile,
a 2-tile, up to a 25-tile CGRA partition to obtain reconﬁg-
uration candidates of each kernel for pipeline rebalancing.
Appropriate loop unrolling should be applied on the DFG
for each mapping as discussed in Section IV-B. Note that the
process may result in several different mappings using the
same number of tiles, i.e., the logical placement/shape of the
partition (and hence, communication links between tiles) may
change depending on how operations are assigned to tiles to
respect data dependencies. The regular shapes (i.e., rows and
columns with a similar number of elements) usually provide
the highest speedups. This happens because each tile in the
regular shape has more neighbors for routing, which facilitates
the heuristic mapping algorithms. Therefore, the algorithm
maps the DFG on the most regular logical shape for each of the
allowed partition sizes. These shapes are selected as logical
placement candidates for dynamic rebalancing (see Fig. 5).
On a 5×5 DRIPS CGRA, each kernel can have up to 25
logical placement candidates. Note that an exhaustive mapping
algorithm can outperform the heuristic one by 10%∼20% in
terms of speedup when a big kernel (e.g., more than 100
DFG nodes) tries to be mapped onto a large CGRA (e.g.,
a 6×8 CGRA) but result in an overhead of 100∼1000 times
on the execution time. However, discussing the performance
of different static mapping algorithms is outside the scope of
this work.
D. Hardware Support
Control Index Preloading – Once all the logical placement
candidates are generated, DRIPS stores all their related control
signals in a dedicated space of the DMA accessible memory.
The corresponding memory addresses for each logical place-
ments are preloaded into the addrTable (shown in Fig. 4) of
the DRIPS Controller. At runtime, the DMA unit can issue
the memory load requests (invoked by the upgrade() and
downgrade() operations) to fetch the corresponding control
signals.
Initial Placement – The initial physical placement of
kernels on the CGRA tiles is determined considering the
representative execution time of each kernel. At runtime, the
placement is changed starting from this initial placement. For
example, the tiles are partitioned into 6 logical placements
(each includes 8, 6, 4, 4, 2, and 1 tile, respectively) to enable
the pipeline of the GCN application. Each logical placement is
physically mapped on the 5×5 CGRA exploring the arrange-
ments in an exhaustive manner, similarly to the loop body of
Algorithm 1. The difference is that for the initial placement
the exploration is performed ofﬂine at compilation, thus not
affecting runtime performance. Once all corresponding control
signals are appropriately loaded, the host processor can invoke
the CGRA execution through the Accelerator Command (see
Fig. 4).
V. EXPERIMENTAL EVALUATION
A. Experimental Setup
DRIPS’ compiler framework is built on top of the
MLIR [15] and the LLVM [16] infrastructures. We use
PyMTL3 [13] to model the DRIPS CGRA [38], DRIPTS
Control Network [36], and the DRIPS Controller at both the
cycle-level and register-transfer-level (RTL). The synthesizable
Verilog is generated from the RTL models to obtain the power,
area, and timing characteristics. The SPM memory is modeled
in CACTI-6.5 [24].
Applications – We evaluate three representative data-
dependent streaming applications (comprising a total of 18
kernels). Graph convolutional networks (GCNs) are an emerg-
ing machine learning model that can be deployed on both
edge devices and servers. We derive a 2-layer GCN model,
used to classify enzymes, from Pytorch-Geometric [9]. In our
experiments, we use a pre-trained model and only consider
inference. We choose the ENZYMES [5] dataset that contains
600 enzymes (450 used for training and 150 used for infer-
ence). The number of nodes in the 600 enzymes ranges from
2 to 126 with an average of 32.6. The different sizes and
structures of the enzymes lead to an imbalanced application
pipeline as discussed in Section II-B. We also implement a
multi-stage raytracing application (Raytracing) that contains 7

Table I shows that the optimal speedup for each kernel
is achieved by mapping the kernel on a subset of the pro-
vided hardware resources. This indicates that sharing resources
among multiple kernels leads to better hardware utilization
and improved overall throughput compared with the sequential
invocation of the kernels (i.e., each kernel solely occupying
all the hardware resources during its execution). Note that
achieving optimal speedup is limited either by the loop-
carried dependencies or actual hardware utilization (e.g., when
mapping and routing complexity does not allow to improve
speedups by just using more hardware resources).
Baseline – We use a statically partitioned CGRA conﬁgura-
tion with the same hardware parameters (i.e., number of tiles
and size of SPM) as the DRIPS architecture for baseline. We
statically partition resources among all kernels proportionally
to their overall average execution times.
B. Experimental Evaluation
Time window – Fig. 10 shows the normalized accumulated
throughput of GCN with different sizes of time window on a
5×5 DRIPS CGRA over the baseline. We can observe that the
smaller time windows (i.e., 1-round, 5-round, and 10-round)
perform better than the larger time windows. This happens
because larger time windows (i.e., more pipeline execution
rounds) might not capture the similarities of a burst of inputs.
On the other hand, the time windows should not be too small:
the DRIPS Controller may in fact not be able to capture
temporal characteristics of the inputs and be misled by noise,
hence resulting in reconﬁgurations that do not produce higher
speedups. As shown in Fig. 10(b), clustering the inputs can
mitigate this phenomenon. For the rest of the experimental
section, we consider a time window of 10-round and randomly
ordered inputs.
Rebalancing overhead – The overhead of the pipeline
rebalancing consists of the reconﬁguration time for updgrad-
ed/downgraded kernels and the control signals transfer time

C. RTL Timing, Area, and Power
We evaluate the timing, area, and power consumption of
our 5×5 DRIPS prototype using the synthesizable Verilog
code generated from our PyMTL3 model. We use Synopsys
Design Compiler, Cadence Innovus, and Synopsys PrimeTime
PX with FreePDK45 in order to synthesize, place, route,
and estimate the power consumption of the designs. We use
CACTI-6.5 [24] to estimate the area and power of the 32KB
scratchpad data memory. The entire chip area is 2.07mm2 and
the operating frequency is 800MHz @ 45nm with an average
power consumption of 564.8mW. The DRIPS Controller only
takes 16.34% of the entire CGRA area.
VI. RELATED WORK
Others have investigated CGRAs in the context of stream-
ing applications. [25] performs efﬁcient kernel selection and
sequential kernel mapping onto CGRAs. It seeks to optimize
the communication between the host and the accelerator but
results in resource under-utilization by not providing features
to merge or co-execute selected kernels, limiting the overall
throughput. Chordmap [19] enables concurrent execution of
multiple kernels, leveraging a static spatial-temporal kernel
mapping strategy, and improving throughput. But, similar to
[25], it continues to assume a synchronous dataﬂow graph ex-
ecution with ﬁxed execution times regardless of the input data.
PPA [27] proposes a hardware monitor to check resource avail-
ability and trigger dynamic partitioning of the reconﬁgurable
substrate. This enables active kernels to increase or reduce
their resource utilization leveraging functional units available
in their vicinity. However, [27] focuses on statically generating
a mapping that can be expanded to different rectangular
shapes at runtime without hardware support, resulting in sub-
optimal dynamic mappings. ARENA [37] provides support for
dynamic partial reconﬁguration of CGRAs. Based on the input
data size, rather than execution time, it maps kernels ready
to execute onto a small number of possible conﬁgurations.
The limited number of conﬁgurations (less than 4) restricts
kernel support and limits its usability. [14, 31, 42] present
similar constraints as they propose statically partitioning their
CGRA tiles to a limited group of kernels. Some commercial
CGRA-like solutions, such as the Xilinx Versal’s Adaptable
Intelligent Engines [10] and the Reconﬁgurable Dataﬂow Units
of SambaNova DataScale system [1], are also limited to ﬁxed
resource mapping as they require a static representation of the
dataﬂow application.
Dynamic reconﬁguration of FPGAs has recently gained
renewed attention [41]. For example, Tabula [2] leverages the
Spacetime technology to enable dynamic reconﬁguration of
the logic, memory, and interconnect resources up to eight
times for each user cycle. A user design is automatically
mapped, placed, and routed into the device by the Spacetime
compiler. However, FPGAs are reconﬁgured at the bit-level
and experience long reconﬁguration latency. The conﬁguration
stream (bitstream) size scales with chip size and is transmitted
through reconﬁguration ports with limited bandwidth. [21]
shows that for a speciﬁc task reconﬁguration takes 252560ns,
5.8 times higher than the actual execution time of such task.
While partial reconﬁguration is possible, the compilation steps
for partial bitstream generation must take into consideration
already occupied constrained areas of the device. This results
in a semi-static mapping of kernels, where new kernels must
match the pre-generated bitstreams.
VII. CONCLUSION
This paper proposes DRIPS — a novel CGRA architecture
that can dynamically rebalance the pipeline of data-dependent
streaming applications. We present the software and hardware
components required to manage partial dynamic reconﬁgura-
tion. DRIPS improves the overall throughput by 1.46× across
a set of representative applications over a statically partitioned
solution. The additional area overhead to enable dynamic
rebalancing consumes 16.34% of the entire area for a 5×5
CGRA prototype.

DRIPS: Dynamic Rebalancing of Pipelined
Streaming Applications on CGRAs
Cheng Tan†, Nicolas Bohm Agostini†, Tong Geng†, Chenhao Xie†, Jiajia Li§, Ang Li†,
Kevin J. Barker†, Antonino Tumeo†
†Paciﬁc Northwest National Laboratory, §William & Mary
†{cheng.tan, nicolas.agostini, tong.geng, chenhao.xie, ang.li, kevin.barker, antonino.tumeo}@pnnl.gov,
§jli49@wm.edu
Abstract—Coarse-grained reconﬁgurable arrays (CGRAs) pro-
vide higher ﬂexibility than application-speciﬁc integrated circuits
(ASICs) and higher efﬁciency than ﬁne-grained reconﬁgurable
devices such as Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs).
However, CGRAs are generally designed to support ofﬂoading of
a single kernel. While the CGRA design, based on communicating
functional units, appears to naturally suit data streaming appli-
cations composed of multiple cooperating kernels, current ap-
proaches only statically partition the resources across application
kernels. However, emerging streaming applications at the edge
(scientiﬁc instruments, sensor networks, network processing)
perform much more than digital signal processing and often are
data and input dependent. This leads to extremely variable kernel
execution times, severely impacting the throughput of the entire
pipeline if resources are only statically allocated. Therefore, in
this paper, we propose DRIPS — a novel CGRA architecture
that can dynamically rebalance the pipeline of data-dependent
streaming applications. We present a uniﬁed compiler framework
to facilitate the mapping of a given streaming application onto
the DRIPS CGRA architecture. The experimental results show
that DRIPS achieves an average throughput improvement of
1.46× across a set of representative applications over a statically
partitioned solution. The additional area overhead to enable
dynamic rebalancing consumes 16.34% of the entire area for
a 5×5 CGRA prototype.
I. INTRODUCTION
Data stream processing applications, which apply complex
sequences of computational kernels to continuously generat-
ed/acquired data, are becoming ubiquitous. The Internet of
Things (IoT), data centers, and edge computing devices for
many different areas require very specialized accelerators to
deal with quickly changing streams of data, and typically
compute results with low latency under very speciﬁc power
constraints. For example, smart sensor networks (power grid,
urban pollution, sea monitoring) need very power-efﬁcient
ways to reduce data to transmit across relatively slow com-
munication links. Other systems, like large scientiﬁc instru-
ments (particle accelerators, electron microscopes) capture and
stream inordinate amounts of data that cannot be stored com-
pletely [4, 32] and thus require high throughput and extremely
low processing latency. One speciﬁc characteristic of these
data streams is that their characteristics are highly variable,
leading to highly different execution times for each of the
computational kernels of the processing pipeline.
This work was performed while Jiajia Li was afﬁliated with Paciﬁc
Northwest National Laboratory.
Accelerators based on reconﬁgurable devices have been
successfully employed to accelerate streaming applications,
speciﬁcally targeting image processing, convolutional neural
networks, and deep learning [3, 11, 27, 29, 34, 35, 45]. The
conventional approach focuses on maximizing the overall
throughput of the applications by accelerating all kernels si-
multaneously. Coarse-grained reconﬁgurable arrays (CGRAs),
loosely deﬁned as sets of functional units or interconnected
by a communication network on which a compiler can map
complex computational patterns deciding how the data ﬂows
through the system, appear a promising class of accelerators
for these speciﬁc application domains. Commonly used today
for digital signal processing (DSP) and, more in general,
multimedia applications [22, 40], they provide adaptability to
evolving algorithmic approaches thanks to their conﬁgurabil-
ity, but are more efﬁcient and faster to reconﬁgure than ﬁne-
grained conﬁgurable devices (such as ﬁeld-programmable gate
arrays). Their reﬁned communication networks enable efﬁcient
data communication from one processing element to the other,
allowing to deﬁne complex data processing pipelines with high
throughputs.
Although CGRAs seem appropriate for applications com-
posed of several communicating kernels, they are generally
programmed in two ways to support data streaming. The ﬁrst
approach uses a CGRA in its entirety for a single kernel
at a time and is reconﬁgured

chunk-57a3a682d8c8b22c568166b68710bb5e.content:
mable gate
arrays). Their reﬁned communication networks enable efﬁcient
data communication from one processing element to the other,
allowing to deﬁne complex data processing pipelines with high
throughputs.
Although CGRAs seem appropriate for applications com-
posed of several communicating kernels, they are generally
programmed in two ways to support data streaming. The ﬁrst
approach uses a CGRA in its entirety for a single kernel
at a time and is reconﬁgured for each subsequent kernel.
In such a situation, if a kernel is small and does not have
enough parallelism, CGRA’s resources may remain unused.
In the second case, the CGRA is statically partitioned to
carefully assign hardware resources to all the kernels of the
application at the same time. Unfortunately, this works well
only if the performance (execution delay) of each kernel does
not change when the characteristics of the input data vary.
For example, edge devices in highly dynamic sensor networks
(monitoring stations for the power grid or pollution, or quickly
moving autonomous drones) may present sudden changes in
the sensed data when the environment changes that produce
a high variation of the execution times for the data ﬁltering
kernels, and consequently inﬂuence subsequent classiﬁcation
stages. Edge devices for modern scientiﬁc instruments need
to quickly reduce (ﬁlter, sparsify, and/or compress) the input
data to enable single or few shots classiﬁcation, but the input
samples (i.e., the data coming from the actual sensing devices)
and their organization can greatly vary from an experiment to

the other. This leads to a highly variable degree of sparsity of
the data structures that then results in variable times for each
one of the kernels in the rest of the processing pipeline. Hence,
statically partitioning the CGRA may lead to different kernels
becoming the bottleneck in the processing pipeline, lowering
the overall throughput, and make the non-bottleneck kernels
underutilize the assigned resources.
To address these shortcomings, we propose DRIPS, a novel
CGRA design that enables Dynamic Rebalancing of pIPelined
Streaming applications. DRIPS implements partial dynamic
reconﬁguration based on the current pipeline execution status
to maximize the overall throughput. The speciﬁc contributions
of this paper are as follows:
• We propose DRIPS, a novel CGRA architecture (Sec-
tion III) that can dynamically rebalance the pipeline of
streaming applications based on the execution status to
achieve the maximized throughput;
• To facilitate the dynamic rebalancing, we develop the
ﬁrst uniﬁed framework (Section IV) to generate the
conﬁguration candidates of each kernel that will be used
to partially reconﬁgure the CGRA at runtime.
• We use a set of representative data-dependent streaming
applications to evaluate DRIPS (Section V). A 5×5
DRIPS prototype is implemented at both the cycle-level
and the register-transfer-level (RTL) to obtain perfor-
mance, power, area, and timing metrics.
II. BACKGROUND AND MOTIVATION
As brieﬂy introduced, CGRAs are either designed to support
the ofﬂoading of a single kernel in the application at a
time or statically partitioned among multiple communicating
kernels. The second approach seems more suitable for data
streaming applications to achieve the optimal throughput, but
requires kernels whose execution times do not change with
the characteristics of the data. The varying data characteristics
lead to sequences of kernels that can present signiﬁcantly
different execution times depending on the speciﬁc inputs.
Some examples are data analysis pipelines that perform graph
representation learning (i.e., graph neural networks, GNNs
[46]). GNNs organize their inputs in graph structures, which
may have signiﬁcantly varying features (not only the scale,
or size of the graphs in terms of the number of vertices,
but also their edge distribution, or sparsity), consequently
requiring very different processing times. Graph convolutional

execution times depending on the speciﬁc inputs.
Some examples are data analysis pipelines that perform graph
representation learning (i.e., graph neural networks, GNNs
[46]). GNNs organize their inputs in graph structures, which
may have signiﬁcantly varying features (not only the scale,
or size of the graphs in terms of the number of vertices,
but also their edge distribution, or sparsity), consequently
requiring very different processing times. Graph convolutional
networks (GCNs) are speciﬁc instances of GNNs where the
propagation module is a convolution operator that transforms
the graph into the spectral domain and aggregates information
from neighbors of vertices. In this section, we discuss the
acceleration on CGRA of a simple, but critical data-dependent
kernel of GCNs, sparse matrix-vector multiplication (SpMV)
in Section II-A and provide a full motivational example for
DRIPS with a GCN in Section II-B.

B. Throughput-Oriented Acceleration for Streaming Applica-
tions
CGRAs, characterized by data ﬂowing from one processing
element to the other, have been demonstrated to be able
to reach high throughput for streaming applications. Xil-
inx Adaptable Intelligent (AI) Engines [10] in the Versal
device and the SambaNova Reconﬁgurable Dataﬂow Unit
(RDU) [1, 44] are two prominent examples of dataﬂow design
where a streaming application composed of multiple kernels is
represented in a single dataﬂow graph and remains ﬁxed during
its execution independently from variations of the input data.
Such a static hardware allocation approach does not allow the
accelerators to react to the variations of the inputs, leading
to an unbalanced pipeline and a reduction of the overall
throughput. In the following paragraphs, we use a GCN as
a motivating example to illustrate the effects of the variation
of the inputs and to explain how DRIPS improves the overall
throughput by dynamically

dataﬂow graph and remains ﬁxed during
its execution independently from variations of the input data.
Such a static hardware allocation approach does not allow the
accelerators to react to the variations of the inputs, leading
to an unbalanced pipeline and a reduction of the overall
throughput. In the following paragraphs, we use a GCN as
a motivating example to illustrate the effects of the variation
of the inputs and to explain how DRIPS improves the overall
throughput by dynamically rebalancing the pipeline of the
computational kernels.
Fig. 2(a) shows a 2-layer GCN used to classify a series
of enzymes (represented as graphs). The ﬁrst layer of the
network consists of an aggregate kernel (Aggregate1), a com-
bine kernel, and a ReLU kernel. The ReLU kernel is merged
with the combine kernel (CombineReLU) through loop fusion
because it is much smaller than the others (this is normally
done by machine learning compiler frameworks, such as
MLIR [15] or TVM [6]). The second layer is composed of an
aggregate kernel (Aggregate2), a combine kernel (Combine),
and a pooling kernel (Pooling).

The input graph of the GCN is usually represented as an
adjacency matrix that is stored in a compressed format (e.g.,
CSR, CSC, COO, etc) [18, 30] because of its inherent high
degree of sparsity (Compress). The aggregate kernels in GCN
collect multiple feature vectors from source neighbors into
one single feature vector and are executed through a sparse
matrix-matrix multiplication (SpMM). As shown in Fig. 1,
the execution time of the SpMV (i.e., the innermost loop of
the SpMM) varies with the number of nonzeros. On the other
hand, the combine kernels (i.e., Combine and CombineReLU)
perform dense general matrix-matrix multiplications (GEMM)
on the weight and feature matrix. Since the number of nodes
and degree of the edges in the input graphs representing the
enzymes might signiﬁcantly vary, the execution time of the
same kernel in the G

chunk-072a87db7c5dbb5fd3ba6bae2d7254f2.content:
i.e., the innermost loop of
the SpMM) varies with the number of nonzeros. On the other
hand, the combine kernels (i.e., Combine and CombineReLU)
perform dense general matrix-matrix multiplications (GEMM)
on the weight and feature matrix. Since the number of nodes
and degree of the edges in the input graphs representing the
enzymes might signiﬁcantly vary, the execution time of the
same kernel in the GCN may be different depending on the
actual input graph.
To enable high throughput on the CGRA for the GCN
inference, current approaches statically partition the CGRA
resources for each kernel (Fig. 2(c)) rather than allocating
all the resources to each kernel at one time (Fig. 2(b)).
The difference in execution cycles is illustrated in Fig. 3.
Unfortunately, the sparsity of the input matrices (caused by
different numbers of nodes and edges) for aggregate kernels
varies. This leads to an imperfect pipeline limited by different
kernels at different times as shown on the rightmost side of
Fig. 2(c). On the opposite, Fig. 2(d) demonstrates how DRIPS
improves the overall throughput by dynamically rebalancing
the pipeline. To be speciﬁc, the aggregate kernels exhibit
longer execution times on input matrices with high density
(i.e., larger graphs with more nodes and edges). When a
series of large input graphs arrives, Aggregate2 is limiting
the throughput while Compress is not, resources of Compress
could be migrated to Aggregate2 to balance the application
pipeline. That is, Aggregate2 is subject to an upgrade of
resources (Fig. 2(d)), and Compress is subject to a downgrade.
Resources assigned to Compress needs to be reduced by the
same amount of resources added to Aggregate2. In such a
way, the pipeline is rebalanced and the overall throughput
is improved (as shown in Fig. 3, the execution throughput
reduces from 47183 cycles/input to 30346 cycles/input).
III. DRIPS ARCHITECTURE
The overview of the DRIPS system architecture is illustrated
in Fig. 4. The DRIPS CGRA is loosely coupled with a
central processing unit (CPU) and can be invoked through the
accelerator command interface. The data and control signals
can be fed into the CGRA’s data scratchpad memory (SPM)
and control memory through a direct memory access (DMA)
unit. The current DRIPS prototype is composed of 25 (5×5)
tiles.
Novel Architectural Features – Differently from the con-
ventional mesh-based CGRA design, DRIPS tiles are inter-
connected in a king mesh (i.e., 8 directions) topology [20]. At
runtime, 4 directions out of the 8 are conﬁgured to reshape
the physical placement of a kernel into a logical placement
using the same number of tiles (detailed in Section III-C
and demonstrated in Fig. 5). This is powered by the two
additional crossbar switches and the Reconnector module (see
Fig. 6). Note that a logical placement means a mapping
that is statically generated for a kernel at compilation time,
while a physical placement indicates how the tiles form the
logical placement through dynamic rebalancing at runtime.
Moreover, DRIPS tiles integrate an additional network to
communicate control signals and execution status (presented in
Section III-B). As shown in Fig. 4(c), the components in gray
identify conventional components of a CGRA tile. The green
and blue elements, instead, highlight the new components
needed to implement a DRIPS tile. In addition, we propose the
DRIPS Controller to enable the dynamic rebalancing (detailed
in Section III-C).

A. DRIPS Tile
A DRIPS tile consists of a functional unit (FU), three sets of
registers, three crossbar switches, and a control memory. The
functional unit supports all the basic operations (e.g., add, mul,
shift, select, branch, load, store, etc). Tiles perform memory
operations (i.e., load/store) by directly accessing the data
memory (a global banked SPM) through the per-row shared
bus highlighted in yellow in Fig. 4(b). The data generated by a
functional unit are packed in a 34-bit word (32-bit for data, 1-
bit for predicate, and 1-bit for bypassing). Control dependency
is represented as data dependency and supported with partial
predication [12]. Each control signal is a word of 29 bits. A
14-bit ﬁeld is used to conﬁgure the functional unit at each
cycle, the other 15-bit ﬁeld contains routing information for
the 6×7 crossbar switch. The total size of the control memory
is 480 bytes, which is large enough for any kernel evaluated
in this work. At runtime, if a tile needs to be reallocated or its
interconnection modiﬁed to support a different mapping of a
kernel (reshaping of the logical placement), its control memory
is updated through the DRIPS Control Network (highlighted in
green in Fig. 4(c)). In addition, the Reconnector reconﬁgures
the 8×4 and 4×8 crossbar switches to effectively form the
new kernel logical placement as shown in Fig. 6.
B. DRIPS Control Network
The DRIPS Control Network transports the control signals
(29 bits), execution status (i.e., a 1-bit termination signal from
a tile and a 1-bit launch signal from the DRIPS Controller),
and the connection information to form a kernel logical place-
ment (i.e., 12 bits for each crossbar switch in a tile, for a total
of 24 bits). Therefore, the total size of a packet delivered by the
DRIPS Control Network is 55 bits. The network implements
an X-Y routing strategy to avoid deadlocks. Once a kernel
completes (i.e., a functional unit executes a branch that breaks
out the loop), the termination notiﬁcation is packaged up and
sent to the DRIPS Controller through the Control Network,
subsequently starting the execution of the dependent kernels.
C. DRIPS Controller
The DRIPS Controller keeps collecting the execution status
of each kernel in the exeTable by receiving the termination
signals from the DRIPS Control Network (Figure 4(d)). Mean-
while, the mapping between the kernels and the tiles is stored
and constantly updated in the mapTable. The depTable, resets
every time a new application starts, records the application’s
kernels dependencies, and issues launch requests to the DRIPS
Control Network. Once the pipeline is rebalanced (i.e., a
kernel is upgraded or downgraded), the DMA unit fetches the
corresponding

while, the mapping between the kernels and the tiles is stored
and constantly updated in the mapTable. The depTable, resets
every time a new application starts, records the application’s
kernels dependencies, and issues launch requests to the DRIPS
Control Network. Once the pipeline is rebalanced (i.e., a
kernel is upgraded or downgraded), the DMA unit fetches the
corresponding control signals, assembles the control packets,
and issues them to the appropriate tiles via the DRIPS Control
Network.
The DRIPS Controller can trigger application pipeline re-
balancing after collecting the execution information during
a time window. A 10-round time window indicates that the
rebalancing is triggered once the last kernel completes its 10th
execution. We evaluate the effects of the length of the time
window in Section V-B (see Fig. 10).
Rebalancing Mechanism – Algorithm 1 describes the
DRIPS Controller’s operations. The Inputs of the DRIPS
Controller are the execution cycles of kernels (exeTable) in
the previous time window, the resource allocation status table
(mapTable), and the address table (addrTable) that stores the
addresses indexing all the conﬁgurations for each kernel (i.e.,
each conﬁguration leverages a different number of tiles as
detailed in Section IV-C. First, the Controller identiﬁes the
kernels with the most and least execution cycles (line 5-6)
and balances their resource allocation (line 7-8). Note that
both upgrade() and downgrade() start from the central point
as shown in Fig. 5. The upgrade() incorporates the nearest tile
not assigned to the kernel, downgrade() eliminates the farthest
tile currently assigned to the kernel. However, upgrading a
kernel might affect the current mapping of the other kernels
(Fig. 5). Therefore, affected kernels (line 9) are adjusted to
ﬁt in the new layout with the available resources (reshape()
in line 12). Once a tile is allocated by the reshape() opera-
tion, it will not be reallocated to the other affected kernels,
guaranteeing the termination of the rebalancing mechanism.

Forming Logical Placement – Fig. 5 shows how kernels

Note that the bottleneck kernel considers the tiles that are
close to each other to form its target logical placement. This
operation can always be completed within 1000 cycles. On
the other hand, overlapping kernels may end in using the sub-
optimal placement, but this does not signiﬁcantly affect the
overall pipeline throughput as they are not the bottleneck.
Interaction with the Pipeline – The rebalancing mecha-
nism does not stop the pipeline execution as it actually works
on the mapTable, the exeTable, and the depTable to recognize
the appropriate conﬁgurations of the affected kernels. Those
tables indicate the current execution status of the pipeline.
However, loading the corresponding conﬁgurations (i.e., con-
trol signals) of the affected kernels delays the execution of the
dependent kernels. Fortunately, the overhead of transferring
the control signals is negligible compared to the entire pipeline
execution as detailed in Section V-B. In addition, loading
the conﬁguration does not pause the execution of the other
kernels that are running concurrently and independently from
the rebalanced ones. For example, loading the conﬁguration of
the second stage will delay the third stage but will not interfere
with the execution of the ﬁrst stage that will be processing the
next input instance. Loading the control signals for a speciﬁc
kernel can only happen after the current execution and before
its next launch. At that time, there are no valid values in the
allocated tiles or on the routed path. Instead, the SPM holds
the output of the current pipeline stage, ready to be processed
by the following stage. The exeTable records whether a kernel
is running or not.
D. DRIPS Scratchpad Memory
All the DRIPS tiles can access the global banked SPM
through the per-row shared bus. Bank conﬂicts are handled
by increasing execution delays of load/store operations. Since
the computational kernel is accelerated in a data-ﬂow fashion,
the longer latency of (pipelined) memory accesses does not
signiﬁcantly affect the overall speedup. Moreover, the DRIPS
Controller allows at most one memory access operation on
each row at each cycle.
The size of the SPM can signiﬁcantly affect the performance
of streaming applications, as discussed by many works [7, 19,
28, 33, 43]. The current DRIPS prototype contains a 4-bank
32KB data SPM, which is sufﬁcient for all the applications
evaluated in this work and allows supporting double-buffering.

grated framework is composed of four main parts: an MLIR
front-end, the Loop-Level Transformations, LLVM, and the
hardware backend.

B. Loop-Level Transformation
If there is an appropriate dialect to represent a streaming ap-
plication, loop-level transformation can directly be performed
in the MLIR framework. Otherwise, they are performed
through LLVM optimization passes. Transformations are as
follows:
Loop Flattening – if there are multiple nested loops, each
kernel in the rearranged pipeline is ﬂattened into a single-
nested loop [17]. This avoids multiple invocations of the
innermost loop and facilitates DFG Mapping.
Loop Unrolling – kernels with low instruction-level paral-
lelism cannot efﬁciently utilize the computing resources of
a large CGRA. Applying loop unrolling to extract higher
instruction-level parallelism and generate a larger DFG allows
reaching higher speedups if more hardware resources (i.e.
CGRA tiles) are provided [39]. However, this is not guaran-

teed due to the inter-iteration dependency and the mapping
complexity on large CGRAs. As shown in our evaluation
(see Table. I), the best speedup of a kernel on a CGRA
conﬁguration is achieved by applying an appropriate loop
unrolling factor for that conﬁguration. The DRIPS compilation
framework applies different unrolling factors for each kernel
given a speciﬁc size of a CGRA partition. The solutions with
unrolling factors leading to the highest speedup are chosen as
a candidate for pipeline rebalancing at runtime.
C. LLVM Level
DFG Generation – After applying the appropriate loop-
level transformations, the toolchain can generate the DFG of
each kernel. Each DFG node represents one basic operation
corresponding to one instruction in LLVM intermediate repre-
sentation (IR). Control dependencies are supported by convert-
ing them to data dependencies using partial predication [12]
and providing hardware support for phi and branch operations.
DFG Mapping – DRIPS leverages a heuristic method [38]
to quickly map the control and dataﬂow graph (CDFG)
on all the partitions with different numbers of the CGRA
tiles. Speciﬁcally, on a 5×5 DRIPS CGRA, the compilation
framework could map the DFG of a kernel onto a 1-tile,
a 2-tile, up to a 25-tile CGRA partition to obtain reconﬁg-
uration candidates of each kernel for pipeline rebalancing.
Appropriate loop unrolling should be applied on the DFG
for each mapping as discussed in Section IV-B. Note that the
process may result in several different mappings using the
same number of tiles, i.e., the logical placement/shape of the
partition (and hence, communication links between tiles) may
change depending on how operations are assigned to tiles to
respect data dependencies. The regular shapes (i.e., rows and
columns with a similar number of elements) usually provide
the highest speedups. This happens because each tile

for each mapping as discussed in Section IV-B. Note that the
process may result in several different mappings using the
same number of tiles, i.e., the logical placement/shape of the
partition (and hence, communication links between tiles) may

maps the DFG on the most regular logical shape for each of the

On a 5×5 DRIPS CGRA, each kernel can have up to 25
logical placement candidates. Note that an exhaustive mapping
algorithm can outperform the heuristic one by 10%∼20% in
terms of speedup when a big kernel (e.g., more than 100
DFG nodes) tries to be mapped onto a large CGRA (e.g.,
a 6×8 CGRA) but result in an overhead of 100∼1000 times
on the execution time. However, discussing the performance
of different static mapping algorithms is outside the scope of
this work.
D. Hardware Support
Control Index Preloading – Once all the logical placement
candidates are generated, DRIPS stores all their related control
signals in a dedicated space of the DMA accessible memory.
The corresponding memory addresses for each logical place-
ments are preloaded into the addrTable (shown in Fig. 4) of
the DRIPS Controller. At runtime, the DMA unit can issue
the memory load requests (invoked by the upgrade() and
downgrade() operations) to fetch the corresponding control
signals.
Initial Placement – The initial physical placement of
kernels on the CGRA tiles is determined considering the
representative execution time of each kernel. At runtime, the
placement is changed starting from this initial placement. For
example, the tiles are partitioned into 6 logical placements
(each includes 8, 6, 4, 4, 2, and 1 tile, respectively) to enable
the pipeline of the GCN application. Each logical placement is
physically mapped on the 5×5 CGRA exploring the arrange-
ments in an exhaustive manner, similarly to the loop body of
Algorithm 1. The difference is that for the initial placement
the exploration is performed ofﬂine at compilation, thus not
affecting runtime performance. Once all corresponding control
signals are appropriately loaded, the host processor can invoke
the CGRA execution through the Accelerator Command (see
Fig. 4).
V. EXPERIMENTAL EVALUATION
A. Experimental Setup
DRIPS’ compiler framework is built on top of the
MLIR [15] and the LLVM [16] infrastructures. We use
PyMTL3 [13] to model the DRIPS CGRA [38], DRIPTS
Control Network [36], and the DRIPS Controller at both the
cycle-level and register-transfer-level (RTL). The synthesizable
Verilog is generated from the RTL models to obtain the power,
area, and timing characteristics. The SPM memory is modeled
in CACTI-6.5 [24].
Applications – We evaluate three representative data-
dependent streaming applications (comprising a total of 18
kernels). Graph convolutional networks (GCNs) are an emerg-
ing machine learning model that can be deployed on both
edge devices and servers. We derive a 2-layer GCN model,
used to classify enzymes, from Pytorch-Geometric [9]. In our
experiments, we use a pre-trained model and only consider

Table I shows that the optimal speedup for each kernel
is achieved by mapping the kernel on a subset of the pro-
vided hardware resources. This indicates that sharing resources
among multiple kernels leads to better hardware utilization
and improved overall throughput compared with the sequential
invocation of the kernels (i.e., each kernel solely occupying
all the hardware resources during its execution). Note that
achieving optimal speedup is limited either by the loop-
carried dependencies or actual hardware utilization (e.g., when
mapping and routing complexity does not allow to improve
speedups by just using more hardware resources).
Baseline – We use a statically partitioned CGRA conﬁgura-
tion with the same hardware parameters (i.e., number of tiles
and size of SPM) as the DRIPS architecture for baseline. We
statically partition resources among all kernels proportionally
to their overall average execution times.
B. Experimental Evaluation
Time window – Fig. 10 shows the normalized accumulated
throughput of GCN with different sizes of time window on a
5×5 DRIPS CGRA over the baseline. We can observe that the
smaller time windows (i.e., 1-round, 5-round, and 10-round)
perform better than the larger time windows. This happens
because larger time windows (i.e., more pipeline execution
rounds) might not capture the similarities of a burst of inputs.
On the other hand, the time windows should not be too small:
the DRIPS Controller may in fact not be able to capture
temporal characteristics of the inputs and be misled by noise,
hence resulting in reconﬁgurations that do not produce higher
speedups. As shown in Fig. 10(b), clustering the inputs can
mitigate this phenomenon. For the rest of the experimental
section, we consider a time window of 10-round and randomly
ordered inputs.

rebalancing consists of the reconﬁguration time for updgrad-

accelerate a large application with more kernels or multiple
applications concurrently. This allows maintaining the good
scalability found in the relatively smaller CGRAs on the larger
ones.
C. RTL Timing, Area, and Power
We evaluate the timing, area, and power consumption of
our 5×5 DRIPS prototype using the synthesizable Verilog
code generated from our PyMTL3 model. We use Synopsys
Design Compiler, Cadence Innovus, and Synopsys PrimeTime
PX with FreePDK45 in order to synthesize, place, route,
and estimate the power consumption of the designs. We use
CACTI-6.5 [24] to estimate the area and power of the 32KB
scratchpad data memory. The entire chip area is 2.07mm2 and
the operating frequency is 800MHz @ 45nm with an average
power consumption of 564.8mW. The DRIPS Controller only
takes 16.34% of the entire CGRA area.
VI. RELATED WORK
Others have investigated CGRAs in the context of stream-
ing applications. [25] performs efﬁcient kernel selection and
sequential kernel mapping onto CGRAs. It seeks to optimize
the communication between the host and the accelerator but
results in resource under-utilization by not providing features
to merge or co-execute selected kernels, limiting the overall
throughput. Chordmap [19] enables concurrent execution of
multiple kernels, leveraging a static spatial-temporal kernel
mapping strategy, and improving throughput. But, similar to
[25], it continues to assume a synchronous dataﬂow graph ex-
ecution with ﬁxed execution times regardless of the input data.
PPA [27] proposes a hardware monitor to check resource avail-
ability and trigger dynamic partitioning of the reconﬁgurable
substrate. This enables active kernels to increase or reduce
their resource utilization leveraging functional units available
in their vicinity. However, [27] focuses on statically generating
a mapping that can be expanded to different rectangular
shapes at runtime without hardware support, resulting in sub-
optimal dynamic mappings. ARENA [37] provides support for
dynamic partial reconﬁguration of CGRAs. Based on the input
data size, rather than execution time, it maps kernels ready
to execute onto a small number of possible conﬁgurations.
The limited number of conﬁgurations (less than 4) restricts
kernel support and limits its usability. [14, 31, 42] present
similar constraints as they propose statically partitioning their

chunk-80f80a02f12f4e7d9000fea69ce18385.content:
dynamic mappings. ARENA [37] provides support for
dynamic partial reconﬁguration of CGRAs. Based on the input
data size, rather than execution time, it maps kernels ready
to execute onto a small number of possible conﬁgurations.
The limited number of conﬁgurations (less than 4) restricts
kernel support and limits its usability. [14, 31, 42] present
similar constraints as they propose statically partitioning their
CGRA tiles to a limited group of kernels. Some commercial
CGRA-like solutions, such as the Xilinx Versal’s Adaptable
Intelligent Engines [10] and the Reconﬁgurable Dataﬂow Units
of SambaNova DataScale system [1], are also limited to ﬁxed
resource mapping as they require a static representation of the
dataﬂow application.
Dynamic reconﬁguration of FPGAs has recently gained
renewed attention [41]. For example, Tabula [2] leverages the
Spacetime technology to enable dynamic reconﬁguration of
the logic, memory, and interconnect resources up to eight
times for each user cycle. A user design is automatically
mapped, placed, and routed into the device by the Spacetime
compiler. However, FPGAs are reconﬁgured at the bit-level
and experience long reconﬁguration latency. The conﬁguration
stream (bitstream) size scales with chip size and is transmitted
through reconﬁguration ports with limited bandwidth. [21]
shows that for a speciﬁc task reconﬁguration takes 252560ns,
5.8 times higher than the actual execution time of such task.
While partial reconﬁguration is possible, the compilation steps
for partial bitstream generation must take into consideration
already occupied constrained areas of the device. This results
in a semi-static mapping of kernels, where new kernels must
match the pre-generated bitstreams.
VII. CONCLUSION
This paper proposes DRIPS — a novel CGRA architecture
that can dynamically rebalance the pipeline of data-dependent
streaming applications. We present the software and hardware
components required to manage partial dynamic reconﬁgura-
tion. DRIPS improves the overall throughput by 1.46× across
a set of representative applications over a statically partitioned
solution. The additional area overhead to enable dynamic
rebalancing consumes 16.34% of the entire area for a 5×5
CGRA prototype.
